questions.question: How do you read the current value of a signal named 'count' in an Angular template or in TypeScript?
options: count.value
count.getValue()
count() — call it as a function
signal.read(count)

questions.question: You have items = signal<string[]>([]). You want to add a new item. Which approach is correct?
options: items().push('new item') — mutate the array returned by the signal
items.value = [...items(), 'new item'] — set the .value property
items.update(list => [...list, 'new item']) — return a new array from update()
items.set(items() + 'new item') — concatenate with set()

questions.question: What is the key difference between computed() and a regular getter?
options: computed() is asynchronous; a getter is synchronous
computed() memoises its result and only recalculates when its signal dependencies change; a getter recalculates on every read
computed() requires a subscription; a getter does not
Regular getters cannot read signals; computed() is the only way

questions.question: When does effect() re-run?
options: On every Angular change detection cycle
Only when you call effect.run() manually
Whenever any signal read inside the effect changes
Once per second by default, configurable via the interval option

questions.question: When should you prefer RxJS Observables over Signals?
options: For simple counter values — signals are too complex for that
For asynchronous streams like HTTP requests, WebSockets, and complex event pipelines
Whenever you need to display a value in the template
RxJS should always be preferred — signals are an experimental feature
